The patient is a 75 year old female who presents for a pre-operative visit. The planned procedure is a LEFT TOTAL KNEE
Patient presents to clinic today for preoperative evaluation in anticipation of left total knee arthroplasty. She has insidious
onset of knee pain. She cannot take stairs due to the pain. She cannot walk more than 200 m at a time due to knee pain.
She has attempted nonsteroidal anti-inflammatories without success. She has attempted a therapeutic exercise program
Head, ears, eyes, nose, and throat: Atraumatic and normocephalic.
Alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation.
Skin: No open lesions, rashes or wounds. No café-au-lait spots. No dark or hairy patches present.
Vascuiar: Normai distal puises all limbs. Capillary refill less than 2 seconds all limbs. No adenopathy present. No stasis
changes about ankles. Normal finger and toenails.
Neurologic: Cranial nerves II - XII grossly intact. Light touch intact all limbs. Motor exam is 5/5 upper and lower extremities.
Gait pattern Is antalgic toward the left bearing full weight. Not using a cane, crutch or walker.
HIp: Limb lengths are symmetric. Nontender over the greater trochanter. No flexion contracture present. Nontender about
either buttock, thigh or knee to palpation. Nontender about the lumbar spine. Negative straight leg raise.
Left knee examination shows that there is a 3 degree varus deformity. There is a slight effusion. There is patellofemoral
Radiographs of the left knee were obtained and reviewed with the patient varus tricompartmental osteoarthritis. Bone-on-
bone in the medial compartment. Large osteophytes present. 4 of 4 changes present.
Left knee osteoarthritis, varus, chronic, severe. The patient has exhausted the nonoperative measures available for pain
control. Pain is interfering with activities of daily living.
The patient has been counseled on the indications risks and benefits of total knee arthroplasty. After considering the options
available to patient wishes to proceed with the surgery as discussed.
Specific risks discussed included but were not limited to: Death, bleeding, for blood transfusion, possible deep vein
thrombosis, possible pulmonary embolus, possible infection, possible failure of implanted device or devices, possilbe
dislocation, possible injury to unintended structures to include nerves, tendons, ligaments, blood vessels and muscles.
The patient was advised that steps will be taken to minimize these risks and it is impossible to eliminate all risk. Detailed
conversation on specific risks and occurred. Treatment protocol for an infected total joint was discussed in detail. Specifically
the patient was advised showed the joint become infected additional surgery and prolonged medications would be required.
Additionally the patient was advised that in some extreme cases amputation is required to control infections. The patient was
advised that prophylactic antibiotics would be given, and every step known to reduce the risk of infection would be employed
in the course of this treatment plan. The patient was also advised on the protocol for reducing the risk of blood clot
formation. The importance of continued pharmacologic treatment to reduce the risk of blood clot formation after leaving the
hospital was also discussed in detail.
The patient was advised on the recovery timeline and rehabilitation protocols to follow surgery. They were advised that the
typical inpatient hospital stay for a total knee replacement is 3-5 days. They were advised on the goals for discharge from the
hospital. They were advised on the options available after discharge from the hospital. They were advised that inpatient
treatment may need to be continued after discharge in a rehabilitation facility or skilled nursing facility. They were advised
that our goal would be for discharged to home with visiting nursing and visiting physical therapy. Once they were mobile and
able to travel in the community then therapy will to be continued on an outpatient basis, The first followup appointment after
surgery typically occurs on postoperative day #14.
After considering the options available and having been given the opportunity to ask questions the patient appeared to
understand the plan and my recommendations. They were willing to proceed with the surgery as discussed and were willing
to consent for blood transfusion if necessary.
The patient was given a admission packet for the hospital. In the packet included an H&P as well as admission orders and
instructions to the patient for the day of surgery. There were advised to consume no liquids or food by mouth after midnight
the night before the surgery, Medications which could be taken before surgery were advised as well. There were counseled
to discontinue all blood thinners for a minimum of 5 days before surgery.
Reviewed HPI, History and ROS at today's visit.
The dictated information, contained herein, was transcribed with voice recognition software. Although reviewed, the content
Potassium Chloride ER (10MEQ capsule, extend, Oral) Active.
